title: Museums of Fine Art and Their Public
content_html:
Museums of fine art and their public

The fact that people go to the Louvre museum in Paris to see the original painting Mona Lisa when they can see a reproduction anywhere leads us to question some assumptions about the role of museums of fine art in today’s world

A.

One of the most famous works of art in the world is Leonardo da Vinci’s Mona Lisa. Nearly everyone who goes to see the original will already be familiar with it from reproductions, but they accept that fine art is more rewardingly viewed in its original form.

B.

However, if Mona Lisa was a famous novel, few people would bother to go to a museum to read the writer’s actual manuscript rather than a printed reproduction. This might be explained by the fact that the novel has evolved precisely because of technological developments that made it possible to print out huge numbers of texts, whereas oil paintings have always been produced as unique objects. In addition, it could be argued that the practice of interpreting or ‘reading’ each medium follows different conventions. With novels, the reader attends mainly to the meaning of words rather than the way they are printed on the page, whereas the ‘reader’ of a painting must attend just as closely to the material form of marks and shapes in the picture as to any ideas they may signify.

C.

Yet it has always been possible to make very accurate facsimiles of pretty well any fine art work. The seven surviving versions of Mona Lisa bear witness to the fact that in the 16th century, artists seemed perfectly content to assign the reproduction of their creations to their workshop apprentices as regular ‘bread and butter’ work. And today the task of reproducing pictures is incomparably more simple and reliable, with reprographic techniques that allow the production of high-quality prints made exactly to the original scale, with faithful colour values, and even with duplication of the surface relief of the painting.

D.

But despite an implicit recognition that the spread of good reproductions can be culturally valuable, museums continue to promote the special status of original work. Unfortunately, this seems to place severe limitations on the kind of experience offered to visitors.

E.

One limitation is related to the way the museum presents its exhibits. As repositories of unique historical objects, art museums are often called ‘treasure houses’. We are reminded of this even before we view a collection by the presence of security guards, attendants, ropes and display cases to keep us away from the exhibits. In many cases, the architectural style of the building further reinforces that notion. In addition, a major collection like that of London’s National Gallery is housed in numerous rooms, each with dozens of works, any one of which is likely to be worth more than all the average visitor possesses. In a society that judges the personal status of the individual so much by their material worth, it is therefore difficult not to be impressed by one’s own relative ‘worthlessness’ in such an environment.

F.

Furthermore, consideration of the ‘value’ of the original work in its treasure house setting impresses upon the viewer that, since these works were originally produced, they have been assigned a huge monetary value by some person or institution more powerful than themselves. Evidently, nothing the viewer thinks about the work is going to alter that value, and so today’s viewer is deterred from trying to extend that spontaneous, immediate, self-reliant kind of reading which would originally have met the work.

G.

The visitor may then be struck by the strangeness of seeing such diverse paintings, drawings and sculptures brought together in an environment for which they were not originally created. This ‘displacement effect’ is further heightened by the sheer volume of exhibits. In the case of a major collection, there are probably more works on display than we could realistically view in weeks or even months.

H.

This is particularly distressing because time seems to be a vital factor in the appreciation of all art forms. A fundamental difference between paintings and other art forms is that there is no prescribed time over which a painting is viewed. By contrast, the audience encourage an opera or a play over a specific time, which is the duration of the performance. Similarly novels and poems are read in a prescribed temporal sequence, whereas a picture has no clear place at which to start viewing, or at which to finish. Thus art works themselves encourage us to view them superficially, without appreciating the richness of detail and labour that is involved.

I.

Consequently, the dominant critical approach becomes that of the art historian, a specialised academic approach devoted to ‘discovering the meaning’ of art within the cultural context of its time. This is in perfect harmony with the museum s function, since the approach is dedicated to seeking out and conserving ‘authentic’, original, readings of the exhibits. Again, this seems to put paid to that spontaneous, participators criticism which can be found in abundance in criticism of classic works of literature, but is absent from most art history.

J.

The displays of art museums serve as a warning of what critical practices can emerge when spontaneous criticism is suppressed. The museum public, like any other audience, experience art more rewardingly when given the confidence to express their views. If appropriate works of fine art could be rendered permanently accessible to the public by means of high-fidelity reproductions, as literature and music already are, the public may feel somewhat less in awe of them. Unfortunately, that may be too much to ask from those who seek to maintain and control the art establishment.

questions.text: perhaps because the availability of novels has depended on _________
options: A. they were once more difficult to see.
B. they were once more valued by society.
C. they lose part of their meaning in a museum.
D. they were once more easily understood.
questions.question: perhaps because the availability of novels has depended on _________
questions.groupHtml:
Questions 27–31

Complete the summary using the list of phrases, A–L, below.
questions.blockTitle: The value attached to original works of art
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
“availability of novels”

Bước 2: Tìm đoạn văn
Đoạn:

“the novel has evolved precisely because of technological developments that made it possible to print out huge numbers of texts”

Bước 3: Phân tích
“print out huge numbers” = sản xuất hàng loạt.
=> Đáp án: mass production

_blockConfig.noteTitle: The value attached to original works of art
_blockConfig.noteContent:
People go to art museums because they accept the value of seeing an original work of art. But they do not go to museums to read original manuscripts of novels, perhaps because the availability of novels has depended on [27] for so long, and also because with novels, the [28] are the most important thing.
However, in historical times artists such as Leonardo were happy to instruct [29] to produce copies of their work and these days new methods of reproduction allow excellent replication of surface relief features as well as colour and [30]. 
It is regrettable that museums still promote the superiority of original works of art, since this may not be in the interests of the [31].

questions.questionHtml:
The value attached to original works of art

People go to art museums because they accept the value of seeing an original work of art. But they do not go to museums to read original manuscripts of novels, perhaps because the availability of novels has depended on 27_______ for so long, and also because with novels, the 28_______ are the most important thing.

However, in historical times artists such as Leonardo were happy to instruct 29_______ to produce copies of their work and these days new methods of reproduction allow excellent replication of surface relief features as well as colour and 30_______.

It is regrettable that museums still promote the superiority of original works of art, since this may not be in the interests of the 31_______.

questions.text: and also because with novels, the _______
options: A. are always misleading.
B. reflect the priorities of the institution.
C. are designed to be as neutral as possible.
D. help people to understand the history of art.
questions.question: and also because with novels, the _______
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
“with novels”

Bước 2: Tìm đoạn văn
Đoạn:

“the reader attends mainly to the meaning of words”

Bước 3: Phân tích
“meaning of words” = ý tưởng/nội dung bên dưới.
=> Đáp án: underlying ideas

questions.text: However, in historical times artists such as Leonardo were happy to instruct______
options: A. They are essential for understanding art.
B. They provide a useful historical context.
C. They are more popular than the paintings themselves.
D. They can discourage individual responses to art.
questions.question: However, in historical times artists such as Leonardo were happy to instruct______
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
“produce copies”

Bước 2: Tìm đoạn văn
Đoạn:

“assign the reproduction of their creations to their workshop apprentices”

Bước 3: Phân tích
“workshop apprentices” = người phụ việc/trợ lý.
=> Đáp án: assistants

questions.text: these days new methods of reproduction allow excellent replication of surface relief features as well as colour and _________
options: A. express concern about the future of art.
B. suggest that museums should be free of charge.
C. explain why museums are so popular.
D. show that art is no longer the main focus.
questions.question: these days new methods of reproduction allow excellent replication of surface relief features as well as colour and _________
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
“surface relief features”, “colour”

Bước 2: Tìm đoạn văn
Đoạn:

“high-quality prints made exactly to the original scale, with faithful colour values”

Bước 3: Phân tích
“original scale” = kích thước gốc.
=> Đáp án: size

questions.text: since this may not be in the interests of the ________
options: A. Reproductions are as good as originals.
B. Reproductions can be seen in their original context.
C. Reproductions allow for a more detailed study of art.
D. Reproductions offer a different but valuable experience.
questions.question: since this may not be in the interests of the ________
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
“not be in the interests of”

Bước 2: Tìm đoạn văn
Đoạn:

“this may not be in the interests of the public”

Bước 3: Phân tích
Museums đề cao tác phẩm gốc nhưng điều này có thể không có lợi cho công chúng.
=> Đáp án: public

questions.text: The writer mentions London’s National Gallery to illustrate
options: the undesirable cost to a nation of maintaining a huge collection of art.
the conflict that may arise in society between financial and artistic values.
the negative effect a museum can have on visitors’ opinions of themselves.
the need to put individual well-being above large-scale artistic schemes.
questions.question: The writer mentions London’s National Gallery to illustrate
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
“National Gallery”, “visitors’ opinions of themselves”

Bước 2: Tìm đoạn văn
Đoạn:

“it is therefore difficult not to be impressed by one’s own relative ‘worthlessness’ in such an environment.”

Bước 3: Phân tích
Tác giả nói môi trường bảo tàng khiến khách tham quan cảm thấy bản thân “worthless” so với giá trị vật chất của các tác phẩm.
=> Đáp án: C

_blockConfig.noteContent:
**A type of parrot:**
- diet consists of fern fronds, various parts of a tree and [1]
- nests are created in [2] where eggs are laid.
**Arrival of settlers**
- the [3] of the kākāpō were used to make clothes.

questions.text: The writer says that today, viewers may be unwilling to criticise a work because
options: they lack the knowledge needed to support an opinion.
they fear it may have financial implications.
they have no real concept of the work’s value.
they feel their personal reaction is of no significance.
questions.question: The writer says that today, viewers may be unwilling to criticise a work because
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
“unwilling to criticise”

Bước 2: Tìm đoạn văn
Đoạn:

“nothing the viewer thinks about the work is going to alter that value”

Bước 3: Phân tích
Người xem cảm thấy ý kiến cá nhân của mình không có ý nghĩa gì trước giá trị đã được xã hội công nhận của tác phẩm.
=> Đáp án: D

questions.text: According to the writer, the ‘displacement effect’ on the visitor is caused by
options: the variety of works on display and the way they are arranged.
the impossibility of viewing particular works of art over a long period.
the similar nature of the paintings and the lack of great works.
the inappropriate nature of the individual works selected for exhibition.
questions.question: According to the writer, the ‘displacement effect’ on the visitor is caused by
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
“displacement effect”

Bước 2: Tìm đoạn văn
Đoạn:

“seeing such diverse paintings, drawings and sculptures brought together…”
“This ‘displacement effect’ is further heightened by the sheer volume of exhibits.”

Bước 3: Phân tích
Hiệu ứng này đến từ việc nhiều loại tác phẩm khác nhau bị gom lại cùng một chỗ và số lượng quá lớn.
=> Đáp án: A

questions.text: The writer says that unlike other forms of art, a painting does not
options: involve direct contact with an audience.
require a specific location for a performance.
need the involvement of other professionals.
have a specific beginning or end.
questions.question: The writer says that unlike other forms of art, a painting does not
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
“painting”, “no prescribed time”

Bước 2: Tìm đoạn văn
Đoạn:

“a picture has no clear place at which to start viewing, or at which to finish.”

Bước 3: Phân tích
Tranh không có điểm bắt đầu hay kết thúc cụ thể như opera, play hay novel.
=> Đáp án: D

questions.text: Art history should focus on discovering the meaning of art using a range of media.
questions.question: Art history should focus on discovering the meaning of art using a range of media.
questions.groupHtml:
Questions 36–40

Do the following statements agree with the claims of the writer in the Reading Passage?

In boxes 36–40 on your answer sheet, write
YES  if the statement agrees with the claims of the writer
NO  if the statement contradicts the claims of the writer
NOT GIVEN  if it is impossible to say what the writer thinks about this
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
“discovering the meaning”, “range of media”

Bước 2: Tìm đoạn văn
Đoạn chỉ nói:

“art historian … devoted to ‘discovering the meaning’ of art…”

Bước 3: Phân tích
Không có thông tin về “using a range of media”.
=> Đáp án: NOT GIVEN

_blockConfig.noteContent:
**A type of parrot:**
- diet consists of fern fronds, various parts of a tree and [1]
- nests are created in [2] where eggs are laid.
**Arrival of settlers**
- the [3] of the kākāpō were used to make clothes.

questions.text: The approach of art historians conflicts with that of art museums.
questions.question: The approach of art historians conflicts with that of art museums.
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
“conflicts”

Bước 2: Tìm đoạn văn
Đoạn:

“This is in perfect harmony with the museum’s function”

Bước 3: Phân tích
“perfect harmony” = hoàn toàn phù hợp, không mâu thuẫn.
=> Đáp án: NO

questions.text: People should be encouraged to give their opinions openly on works of art.
questions.question: People should be encouraged to give their opinions openly on works of art.
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
“encouraged”, “opinions”

Bước 2: Tìm đoạn văn
Đoạn cuối:

“The museum public… experience art more rewardingly when given the confidence to express their views.”

Bước 3: Phân tích
Tác giả cho rằng mọi người nên được khuyến khích bày tỏ quan điểm.
=> Đáp án: YES

questions.text: Reproductions of fine art should only be sold to the public if they are of high quality.
questions.question: Reproductions of fine art should only be sold to the public if they are of high quality.
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
“high quality”

Bước 2: Tìm đoạn văn
Đoạn cuối:

“If appropriate works of fine art could be rendered permanently accessible… by means of high-fidelity reproductions…”

Bước 3: Phân tích
Tác giả nói về việc dùng bản sao chất lượng cao để công chúng tiếp cận nghệ thuật dễ hơn, nhưng không nói “only be sold”.
=> Đáp án: NOT GIVEN

questions.text: In the future, those with power are likely to encourage more people to enjoy art.
questions.question: In the future, those with power are likely to encourage more people to enjoy art.
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
“those with power”, “encourage more people”

Bước 2: Tìm đoạn văn
Đoạn cuối:

“that may be too much to ask from those who seek to maintain and control the art establishment.”

Bước 3: Phân tích
Tác giả cho rằng những người có quyền lực không muốn mở rộng khả năng tiếp cận nghệ thuật cho công chúng.
=> Đáp án: NO
